articles.title: Factors Associated With Poor Outcomes For Female Patients Undergoing Total Shoulder Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Wright MA, Geissbuhler AR, Aleem A, Keener JD, Zmistowski B
articles.population: Female patients undergoing primary anatomic or reverse total shoulder arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 409 women (264 achieved good outcomes, 145 did not)
articles.outcomes: Patient satisfaction, pain scores, shoulder function (ASES scores), ability to perform daily activities at 2 years post-surgery
articles.take_home: Female patients who smoke, have had previous shoulder surgery, or receive reverse shoulder replacement are less likely to have good outcomes. Women are most satisfied when they can perform activities requiring overhead shoulder movement like combing hair and reaching to high shelves.

articles.title: Is There a Long-Term Benefit to use Patient-Specific Guides for Glenoid Implant Positioning in aTSA? A comparative study at minimum 10 years.
articles.authors: Gauci MO, Millet N, Jacquot A, Berhouet J, Bronsard N, Gonzalez JF, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective comparative study based on a prospective cohort
articles.population: Patients undergoing anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 47 patients (27 without patient-specific guides, 20 with patient-specific guides)
articles.outcomes: Revision rates, pain scores, shoulder function, patient satisfaction, radiographic component migration and loosening at minimum 10 years follow-up
articles.take_home: After 10 years, using patient-specific guides for glenoid implant positioning did not improve outcomes, revision rates, pain relief or shoulder function compared to standard surgery. Patient-specific guides may not be necessary for this procedure.

articles.title: ''The Global Glenoid Component Inclination: why scapulothoracic orientation should be considered when defining glenoid component inclination in reverse total shoulder arthroplasty''.
articles.authors: Shekhbihi A, Raiss P, Modelhart M, Randlkofer LM, Herbst EC, Moroder P
articles.population: Patients undergoing reverse total shoulder arthroplasty with 3D preoperative planning
articles.outcomes: Glenoid component inclination relative to gravity, scapular rotation angle, shoulder function scores (ASES Index) at 9 and 26 weeks
articles.take_home: The way a patient's shoulder blade naturally sits varies widely between people, which affects the actual angle of the implant component relative to gravity even when surgeons aim for a standard angle during surgery. Taking each patient's individual shoulder blade position into account during planning could help avoid complications and improve recovery.

articles.title: Morphological analysis of the scapula in healthy and osteoarthritic subjects.
articles.authors: Werthel JD, Ogor J, Le Brigand J, Walch G, Gerber C, Morvan Y, et al.
articles.population: Older adults with and without shoulder osteoarthritis
articles.sample_size: 619 shoulders (134 healthy controls aged over 60 years, 485 with primary osteoarthritis)
articles.outcomes: Differences in glenoid version, inclination and rotation; humeral subluxation; critical shoulder angle; sagittal acromial tilt; posterior acromial coverage and height; and scapular spine angle between healthy and osteoarthritic shoulders and between Walch type A and type B glenoids
articles.take_home: Shoulder osteoarthritis is linked to distinct differences in the shape of the scapula (shoulder blade) compared to healthy shoulders, including changes in bone angles and positioning. The study cannot yet say whether these shape differences cause osteoarthritis or develop because of it, so further research is needed.

articles.title: Impact of Severity of Allergy to Beta-lactam Antibiotics on the Perioperative Use of Cephalosporins and Revision Rates following Total Shoulder Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Fucich D, Kalva S, Joshi T, Perry A, Yao JJ, Virk MS
articles.outcomes: Use of cephalosporin prophylaxis, revision surgery rates, septic and aseptic revisions, intraoperative antibiotic reactions
articles.take_home: Patients with reported beta-lactam allergies were less likely to receive cephalosporin antibiotics during shoulder replacement surgery and had higher rates of needing revision surgery for infection. Most reported beta-lactam allergies appear unreliable, and allergy testing might safely allow more patients to receive the most effective antibiotic protection.

articles.title: Incidence of Lower Extremity Deep Vein Thrombosis Following Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair.
articles.authors: Akimoto K, Ikeda Y, Nakajima F, Hashimoto M, Abe Y, Takuro M, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing arthroscopic rotator cuff repair without preoperative deep vein thrombosis
articles.outcomes: Incidence of lower extremity deep vein thrombosis on postoperative days 3, 7 and 10; perioperative D-dimer levels; independent risk factors for DVT
articles.take_home: Deep vein thrombosis occurred in 5.3% of patients after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, but all cases were asymptomatic and located in distal veins. Being aged 65 years or older and female sex were risk factors, though the study suggests routine screening for this complication may not be necessary given the benign nature of cases found.

articles.title: "Am I too old for a shoulder replacement?" The association between age and comorbidities on the outcomes following Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: O'Malley O, Davies A, Rangan A, Sabharwal S, Reilly P
articles.study_type: Population-based cohort study using national registry data
articles.population: Patients aged 80 years and over undergoing reverse shoulder arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Revision surgery rates, 30-day medical complications, length of stay, and mortality
articles.take_home: Reverse shoulder replacement in very elderly patients has low revision rates, but medical complications and death within a year increase significantly with age and existing health conditions. Age alone should not prevent consideration of the operation, but patients need realistic discussions about these risks beforehand.

articles.title: Scapula-to-scapula tethering for the treatment of scapulothoracic abnormal motion: A novel technique and case series.
articles.authors: Lohre RS, Ahmed AF, Elhassan BT
articles.population: Patients with scapulothoracic abnormal motion (disabling scapular dyskinesis) who had failed non-operative treatment, with intact rotator cuff and deltoid strength of at least 4/5
articles.outcomes: Pain (VAS), function (SSV and ASES score), range of motion (forward elevation and abduction increased; rotation unchanged), graft failures and reoperations
articles.take_home: Scapula-to-scapula tethering improved pain, function and some shoulder movements in patients with disabling scapular dyskinesis, but three patients had late graft failures requiring further surgery. The technique appears promising as an alternative to fusion, though larger studies with comparison groups are needed to confirm its effectiveness.
